segments.text: Welcome to this Autodesk DevCon session towards bringing human experience to design using APS.

and we'll be talking to you for the next 20 or so minutes.
Okay, so here's the agenda. For today we're going to do a quick introduction,
talking a little bit about our area of research.
And then look at two types of experiential walkthroughs that we've run inside research.
One is physical, one is virtual and then we'll spend a little bit of time talking about what's next for us.
So Thomas and I work in a research area called in live and design and our tagline really is about our goal is to bring designs to life
by simulating the human experience of design using AI.

And so a lot of this is around simulation, but clearly a big piece of this is having data about real world experience in order to fuel that simulation and have it grounded in in real experience experience experiential data.
So today we're going to talk about our efforts to capture that data.
So firstly we'll talk about physical experiential walkthroughs.
So this is a really largely around a project that we did with the bentway in Toronto.
So this was the culmination of our efforts with the bentway was a presentation was sorry an exhibition that was held at their offices in the city.

And we had a set up where we had six large screens and then a smaller touch screen where people could interact with the data and explore it and we'll explain the data that was that we effectively collected next.

So in terms of our collaboration with the bendway so this was an opportunity for us to sort of build a nascent technology platform that could process and store multi modal experiential data with the ultimate goal of surfacing insights.
They could feed back into the design process.
So we went to the the bentway and we had a number of people come and experience it and capture their experience of the of the environment.
The bendway is a community space that's beneath the gardener expressway in downtown Toronto. You can see where it runs in the bottom map here in the top left.
You can understand why it's called the bentway because each of these concrete sections that hold up the highway is called a bent.
So it's called the bendway.
All right, so we captured a data from 15 participants who signed up to be part of this this experiment or this study and they came along and they wore these these hard construction helmets that had

a 360 degree camera as a GoPro on top. There was a boom assembly where there was a Raspberry Pi zero and a camera that was effectively capturing their their facial response during during the walkthroughs.
We had environmental sensors capturing data around temperature humidity, etc.
And then there was GPS location that was captured. So this is all the quantitative very easily quantifiable data that we captured per participant.
But there was also data that we captured on the qualitative side.
So we had intermittent part, you know, participant states we asked for data from the the participants virus smartphone app.
We provided them tasks in order to get them to find a good place to read a book in the space, for example.
We also ran surveys throughout and then had an interview as well in terms of capturing this data around how people felt so much more qualitative.
In terms of the data, so here we have just some of them this multimodal input that we captured for people.
So in the top left we have the 360 video footage there we processed it as well to sort of blur out people to make sure there was no so you know we didn't have issues with with privacy.
We segmented the data so run it through some algorithms that the basically performed computer vision and could pull out different types of objects.
So there you have different color schemes for different. So you have the sky you have buildings you have the ground you have cars you have people, etc. etc.
And then all of these relate to each other these different types of objects. So you know the the building is on the ground.
The car is next to the building, etc. So we had this we could build out this rich scene graph relationship graph explaining how everything connects.

We also processed the facial expression data so from we from the selfie cameras we could extract a frame by frame facial expressions and basically track people's emotions at least to some degree as they were expressed.
You know, facially. And of course we had the GPS data so all of this data was was connected to locations throughout their specific walkthroughs.
And this is again a visualization of the data that as you would have seen on those six big screens at the on that first slide with the touch screen at the bottom.
We have multi you know it's multi model data that's different modes of display. You can just show the video you can show the scene graph you can show the segmentation.
The qualitative data is more on the left of the screen. The more quantitative data is shown on the right.
You could do things like scrub forward to a point in the walkthrough where you could see lots of trees or lots of cars to understand what what the surroundings were like at particular point.
So it's a really interesting interactive way to explore this kind of rich data set.
There were some challenges around this however so you know physical data capture is tricky.
You know we had the setup of the devices is quite flaky to have to deal with these these complex rigs to reset them to make sure everything was working.
We we had issues around data transfer.
500 gigabytes of data that we that we captured just for 15 participants and storing that actually there was an interesting work because it was a project that was with a Canadian partner.
We had to make sure that the data stayed in Canada. So that was another interesting challenge as well. Luckily we have an office in Toronto.
Obviously privacy compliance you know making sure that we anonymized the data sufficiently to comply with regulations.

So there was a lot that had to be done in terms of the environment it may seem like a great idea to do experiments in the real world but part of the issue with the physical environment is you can't control it.

So it's difficult to disambiguate whether somebody's having an emotional response to a bird flying over or a car honking its horn or whatever this it's difficult to understand what the reasons were for a response.
It's much more easy to control that in a virtual space for example, which is why so many neuroscience experiments use use virtual reality.
It's also difficult to scale this kind of physical data capture. So we managed to do this for 15 people and it was a lot of effort and a lot of manpower from our side.
But doing the same thing for hundreds or thousands of people at the same location would have been very complicated of course over multiple days different weather conditions.
Everything gets very much more complicated and scaling to multiple environments also is complicated means different travel concerns.
You know moving people around or inviting a different set of participants per location it just becomes really unmanageable.
It was a good experience but we we obviously saw the limits on that front.

So we decided to pivot to focus our attention on the virtual to explore the potential for using virtual reality tools in particular one Autodesk tool the workshop XR which was not a tool designed for stakeholder engagement or experience and capture and analysis is very much more for design team collaboration and having people get together and analyze projects together.
So we wanted to explore whether we could use this technology in a way that allowed us to capture the human experience of the built environment and that's what Thomas will talk about next.

Thank you, Thomas. So just to wrap up, we thought we'd talk a little bit about what we're working on at the moment and where we're hoping to go with it.
So as I mentioned at the beginning, our focus is really around simulating the human experience of design, and in particular the built environment.
So right now we're looking at multi-agent simulation technologies where we can effectively have embodied agents experiencing space and actually having a realistic response to it.

And part of that is building out what we're calling a human cognitive digital twin, which can effectively take in various inputs such as the environment, such as the persona of the particular agent that we're simulating and the goals that they have.

And because your experience of space is very much dictated or influenced by the task that you have in mind. So whether you want to sit down and have a coffee with friends, or if you want to have a quiet place to work or read a book, you're going to look at space in a different way and have different needs and different, you know, your experience will be different.

And effectively part of our effort around capturing data is relating to this, we're also partnering with institutions, academic institutions who are capturing data in a similar way in order to scale this up so that we can get a really nuanced view of how humanity and all its subjectivity and diversity is going to experience space when going through their day to day work and life.

And with that, just like to thank you very much for attending this presentation. We hope you found it useful. And if you'd like to hear more about what we're doing, then please do reach out. Thank you very much.
